items.question: Who uses train, validation, and test splits in an ML project?
items.answer: Data scientists and ML engineers create and enforce splits; stakeholders rely on test-set metrics for go/no-go decisions — everyone benefits when development tuning stays on validation and the test set remains locked.

items.question: What is a train/validation/test split?
items.answer: A partition of labeled data into three disjoint sets: train for fitting parameters, validation for comparing models and hyperparameters during development, and test for a final held-out generalization estimate.

items.question: When should you create splits relative to preprocessing?
items.answer: Before any step that learns statistics from data (scaling, imputation, feature selection) — split first so validation and test rows are never seen during fit() or preprocessing fit().

items.question: Where in the workflow does each split appear?
items.answer: Train data flows into fit(); validation data is scored after each experiment for model selection; test data is scored once at sign-off — both hold-out sets use predict() only, never fit().

items.question: Why have a separate validation set instead of only train and test?
items.answer: Model selection requires many comparisons — if you tune using the test set, it stops being unseen and the final score is optimistically biased. Validation absorbs iterative peeking; test stays honest.

items.question: How do you implement a 70/15/15 stratified split in code?
items.answer: Hold out 30% with stratify=y, then split that remainder 50/50 with stratify again — yielding 70% train, 15% validation, 15% test with preserved class proportions in each partition.

items.question: Validation set vs test set — what is each for?
items.answer: Validation supports repeated model and hyperparameter comparisons during development. Test is evaluated rarely (ideally once) for an unbiased final generalization estimate after all choices are made.

items.question: Random split vs temporal split — when does each apply?
items.answer: Random (stratified) splits suit i.i.d. tabular problems where future rows resemble past rows in any order. Temporal splits train on earlier periods and validate/test on later ones — required when time ordering matters and random shuffling would leak future information.

items.question: Cause: hyperparameters are chosen by best test-set accuracy across 30 trials. Effect?
items.answer: The test set participated in model selection — reported test accuracy overstates true generalization because the split is no longer an honest unseen sample.

items.question: Cause: the same patient appears in both train and test via visit-level random splitting. Effect?
items.answer: Test rows are not independent of training — the model may recognize patient-specific patterns, inflating validation and test metrics beyond what new patients would produce.
